RE: Questions on Conf l-ict of Interest
Dear Dick:
There have arisen two situations where RDA Board
Members have or may have i-nterest in two projects before the
RDA. P1ease advise us as to what these Board Members should
do, should not do, or may do with regards to these two
proj ects .
Case +1
There .r-s a 4-pJ.ex being buitt at 5th & D Streets in
wasco. There is a partnership which is financing and
building this apartment. Being in the WRDA Project Area,
they have asked for a $5,000 - $8,000 financial assi.stance
grant for either excavation, or for sidewalks and driveway
improvements. one of the partners is Board I,lembe r Fred
west, Jr. 
He has not participated in any discussions of the
project. 
He will 
even leave the Board Chambers when the
matter is discussed. However, other board members believe
they may also have conflicts of interest. 
One members
nephew-in-Ia''e is another partner in the project. 
Another
Board Member sold west the land. And another member owns
property next door. 
So 4 of the 5 Board Members are
concerned about their conf licts-of -interest.
The questions about the above are this:
1) 
May the Board take any action on any item or
project where one of their fellow members has a
direct ownership interest? If so, what procedures
must-tEey f o-IIow_ in dealing with the matter?
2) 
As I have described it, do the Board members wiro
do not have a direct interest in the project, have
va flE--conf licts of i.nterest anyway? If not, what
should they do in resolvlng this issue?
7


7:
1) 
Are there any other standards or rules the Board
ought to be aware of when confronting this type of
s.i tuat ion?
Case * 2
This lnvolves a devel-opment by two eye physicj-ans.
They had already received their permit to build when they
approached the RDA to assist in a particularly 
unusual
problem. The phone company, Pac BeLI, said that in order to
move a line pole at the Street which was blocking placement
of a driveway, Pac BeIl would have to be paid in advance by
the owners. It will cost over $8,000 to move the pole. 
The
driveway coul-d only be placed at the designated location to
make the Iot a legally usable lot. 
The owners asked if the
RDA could make the pa)rment through a grant as they had
already received their development loan.
The possible conf lict -of - interest problem is that the
buildj-ng contractor they hired to construct the building is
Board Member Paul NeufeId. Mr. Neuf el-d has never intervened
in the discussions with the physi-cians. He has never
represented them to the city 
or RDA of f icial,s. 
The
replacement of the pole is not a part of Mr. Neufelds
contract for services and will not effect his contract one
way or another.
The question about conf licts-of - interest in this case
l-) 
Should. Board Member Neufeld remove himself from
the meeting in which this matter is discussed?
2) 
Again, can this matter even be acted upon given
Directors Neufeld's contract with the owners?
3 ) 
Are there any other standards or rules the Board
ought to be aware of when confronting this tlpe of
s ituation?
Richard, I hope my explanations of the cases are
thorough enough to be heIpful. 
Of course, we would like
some guj.dance as soon as possible. Please let me know if
you have any questions.
Sincerely yours ,
7l^*"t'/*-
F. Hendrickson
Manager

: Richard Hargrove

SUBIEI: Arnendments to "C.onfllct of Interest" letter
I sent you a letter concerning possible conflicts of interest lnvolving RDA
mernbers. I am including two anrendrnents to that letter.
The arnendments are:
'l) Case # 1, .lst 
paragraph, l.lth 1ine, the sentence should read,
-..-tt.t. .nephew-in-1aw, once renoved, . . .
make the facts nrrre accurate.


FILE COPY
MEMORANDUM
DATE :
FROM i
TO:
RE:
January 24, 19 91
Ton Keene, Office of General Counsel to the Redevelopment
Agency
John Hendrickson, City Manager
Your recent correspondence inquiring about possible
conflict of interest issues
Case # 1
As I understand this matter, a partnership vrhich is
develop.ing a multi-fanily housing uni.t in the project area has
asked the Agency for financial assistance, (A) One of the partners
is a nember of the Agency's Boaxd. (B) One of the other nembers of
the Agency's Board has a nephew-in-1aw, once removed, $rho is
another one of the partners, (C) A third inember of the Board had
owned this sane property but had sold it to member A three Years
ago when neither \,ras on lhe Board or the City Council.
(D) A fourth board member owns land which is next door to the
property which is the subject of the developnent.
1) 
Ii{ay the Board take any action on any item or project where one
of their fe11ow members has a g::99!_!!!9!EE!p interest? rf
so, what procedures must they follow in dealing with the
matter?
short answer: Yes, the board members who do not,
themselvesl-TEiE----?Iiect ownership interest nay participate in
decision-making with regard to the property. unless otherwise
prohibited by larr.
Discussion: The mere fact one or two of their members
has a con-iffEE--6f interest prohibits only that nember fron
participating in the decision. Members who do not have a conflicl
of interest themselves may partlclpate. No special procedures must
be follo\red. Ho$ever, if more than one Board menber has a conflict
then the i.ssue is whether there ale sufficient nernbers on the Board
who do not have a confllct of interest to constitute a quofum of
the Board. If a quolum remains. then that quorum can decide'. If
rln other words, lre do not count the conflicted nember for any
purpose, incfuding the establishment of a quorum. For example, -if
a five member board had two members declare a conflict of interest
and step down, and one of the other three nembers was no present
that night, the remaining two, non-conflicted menbers cannot act on
this agenda item because they lost their quorun when the two grho
had conflicts of j.nterest declared them, The rule of necessity
(continued.., )


John Hendrickson, City Manager
January 24, 1991
Page 2
a quorum would not remain if -all of those with conflicts step down,
then the rule of necessity2 says alI of the members, in-luding
those with conflicts of interest, (whether direct or indirect), 
maY
participate in the decision after having made their oral disclosure
of the conflict of interest.
2l 
As described above, do the Board members who do not have a
direct interest in the project, have valid conflicts 
of
interest anyway? If not, what should they do in resolving
this issue?
Short answer: Board members A and D have a conflict of
interest but Board members B and C do not. If B and C continue to
maintain that they have a conflict of interest then no decision can
be made. The rule of necessity does not apply. If they agree that
they do not, then B, C and the other members of the Board, (other
than A and D), may act. 
However, please read the ans!'rer t.o (3)
below before taking any action.
Discussion: 
In our opinion, based on the following
discussionl--E6Eid--iember A, who is himself a partner, has a
conftict of interest in making a decision as to whether to lend the
partnership money to improve the real propert,y and, so long as he
1(...continued)
would not help under this set of facts, because there are
sufficient board members in office to make a decision, if they were
all present. Therefore, there is no choice but to continue the
matter .
2This rule is the product of FPPC decisions, Attorney General
opinions and some court decisions. It is based on a section of the
Fair PoIiticaI Practices Act which says that the Act,
"does not prevent any public officiaL from making or
participating in the making of a governmental decision to
the extent his participation is Iegally required for the
decision to be made. The fact that an official's 
vote is
needed to break a tie does not make his participation
IegaIIy required for purposes of this section. "
The regulations then interpret this to mean that the rule of
necess.i.ty applies if and only if, 
"there exists no alternative
source of decision consistent with the purposes and terms of the
statute authorizing the decision. " 
The regulation goes on to
specify that this rule is not to be construed to permit an official
vrho has a conflict of interest to vote merely to break a tj.e nor
permit him to vote,
"if a quorum can be convened of other members of the
agency who are not disqualified Iunder the Act] whether
oi not such other members are actually present at the
time of disqualification. "


John Hendrickson, City Manager
January 24, 19 9l
Page 3
is either a partner in a business lrhich owns land in the Project
Area or owns land in the ProJect Area directly, wilf have conflicts
of interest with regard to many of the majol decision involving the
redevelopment proJect. Board member B, probably has no conf.Lict of
interest merely because of the partnership status of the Board
nember's nephew-in_law, once removed. If Board nember c has been
paid for the property in question and has no remaining legal
interest in the property, then Board member c probably has no
conflict of interest either, 
Board member D has a confLict of
interest only tf the loan to A's partnership !.rould have lriIl have
"no financial effect" ,hatsoever on D's property. Since the Loan
to A's partnership witl be used to develop the partnership's Iand,
it is clear that the action will have some financial effect on the
value of D's land, Therefore, D has a conflict of interest'
The term "conflict of lnterest" is nost easily defined by
the Fair Polittcal Practices Act. Section 87100 of the Governnent
Code provides:
"No public officiat at any 1eveI of state or loca]
government shall nake, participate in makinq or ln any
way attenpt to use his officiaL position to lnfluence a
governnental deci.sion in vrhich he kno\rs or has reason to
know he has a financial lnterest."
The tern "pubtic officlal at any level of state or 1oca1
government'i, is defined 1n the legulatlons t{hich are found at TitIe
2, california Administratlve code, Seqtion 18?00, et' seq. The
tern is defined to mean, "every natural person i,ho is a menber,
offlcer, enployee or consultant of a state or local goverrulent
agency.'r Clearty a clty council mernber or a member of the Board of
Directors of a Redevelopnent Agency is a "publlc official" within
this de flnition.
The tern, "nakes a govelnmental decision" is defined to
include voting, appointing a person, enteling into a contract on
behalf of t.he agency or "obllgates or coNnits his or her agency to
any course of aation." It can aLso include a declslon !g! to take
any of these actions, unless the dec-ision noL to act is made
because of the financial interest, "l'lhen the deternination not to
act occurs because of his or her financial j.nterest. the official's
determination must be accompanied by discLosure of the financial
interest, made part of the agency's official record or made in
writing to the, 
." to the appropriate pefson. Again, any formal
action by the Board of the RedeveLopment Agency which affected this
property would probably be considered the making of a governrnental
decls 1on.
The term "attemptinq to use his official position to
influence", is defined in the regulations to include, "for Lhe
purpose of influencing the decision, the offlclal contacts, or


John Hendrickson, City Manager
January 24, 1991
Page 4
appears before, or other wise attempts to influence, any member,
officer, 
employee or consultant of the agency," in which he is an
official 
or over which the agency in which he is an official 
has
budgetary control.
"Attempting to influence", also includes "aPpearance or
contact by the official 
on behalf of a business entity, client, or
customer. " So, in other words, if Board member A, who is a nember
of the partnership which own the property were to even contact
empLoyees or other Board members on behalf of the partnership, he
would be "attempting to use his official position Iwith the Agency]
to influence" a decision which the Agency was making in regard to
the property. 
The language of this regulation is so broad that
many attorneys advise the affected public official to at least step
down from the dias where the other members are discussing the
matter and some even advise the person to leave the room entirely
in order to avoid the appearance of "attempting to influence" the
decis ion.
The term "material financial effect" is defined in the
regulations based on the nature of the interest'
Boardmember A: Under the regulations adopted Pursuant to
the Fair P6ITEGI Practices Act, the decision is said to have a
material financial effect if "any business entity in which the
officlal 
is. 
.Ia] partner. 
.is directly involved in a decision
before the officiat's 
agency." 
If the official 
owned land
directly, then the decision is said to have a material financial
effect if ,
"the decision is to 
adopt the redevelopnent plan,
to add terrltory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind
or amend any of the above decisions; and real property in
which the official 
has an interest, or any part of it is
located within 
the boundaries (or 
the 
proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment area."
Similarly, if the official 
owned the land directly involved in the
decision, then if 
the decision involved the imposition or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed on the property or if
the decision involved the revocation or denial of a license or
permit or land use entitlement authorizing a specific use of the
property then the decision clearly has a material financial effect
on the property.
Boardmember B:
The general rule contained in the
regulations in regard to whether a decision has a "material
financial effect", is that the decision does have such an effect,
"if the decision will have a significant effect on
the official 
or a member of the official's 
immediate


John Hendrickson, City Manager
January 24, I99 L
Page 5
family, or on the source of income, the source of gifts,
the business entity, or the real property, which is an
economic interest of the official."
Additionat provisions state that the effect of a decision is
material if that decisi.on wiII result in the
"personal expenses, income, assets (other than interest
in real property), or liabilities 
of the official 
or his
or her immediate family increasing or decreasing by at
least $250. "
Repeatedly, the regulations, when they refer to anyone other than
the official 
himself or herself, the reference is to "his or her
immediate family. " Unfortunately, the regulations never define vrho
is within an "immediate famiIy". 
The fact that the adJective
"immediate" was used tends to mean to me that someone as remote as
a nephe!,r- in- Iar,r, once renoved, was not intended to be included.
Certainly if the people in question are adults who do not reside in
the same household, this remote a relationship would not come
within the scope of the term "immediate family", in my opinion.
Boardmember D:
Board nember D owns. If it
appIy. If it is not his
You do not say what kind of ProPerty
is his residence then one set of rules
residence then another set applies.
First, I will assume that it $ras not his residence:
A decision is said to have a "material financiaf effect"
on property indirectty involved in the decision lf the effect of
the decision is material as to real property which the official has
a direct interest in and, one of the following applies: (a) the
property is withln a 300 foot radius of the boundaries of the
property whlch is the subject of the decislon, "un.Less the decision
will have no fLnancial effect upon the official's real property
interest". or (b) the real property is outside of the 300 foot
radius but within a 2,500 foot radius and the decision wiII have a
reasonably foreseeable financial effect of $10,000 more on the
market value of the official's land, or of $1,000 or more in annual
rental value of the official's land. If the land is more than
2,500 feet from the subject property then the decision is presumed
by Iaw to have no material financial effect on the official's
property.
If the property is D's residence then a special rule
applies. Because the City has a population of less than 25,000
people and covers a geographic area of ten square miles, or Less,
and the Council members are required to reside in the jurisdiction,
gnq, are elected at large and we are talking about an indirect
benefit to D's property, theh we can ignore the 2,500 foot radius
issue. Hor"/ever, the 300 foot radius would stil1 app1y. Since you


John Hendrickson, City Manager
January 24, 799 7
Page 6
say thaL D's property is "next door", I assume that it is .Less than
300 feet away.
3) 
Are there any other standards or rules the Board ought to be
aware of when confronting this type of situation?
Short answer: Yes.
Discussion: All of the above analysis is based on the
fair politIEEI practices Act and the regulations adopted pursuant
to it. 
There are, however, two other sources of binding 1aw and
regul-ations on the subject of conflicts of interest. First of aI1,
other provisions of the Fair Political Practices Act require the
City to have adopted its ovrn conflict of j.nterest code. Some codes
adopted by cities merely recite provisions of the Act, other have
their own substantive rules. I do not know if your City or the
Agency by itself have adopted any such code.
Secondly, the Government Code contains another chapter on
conflicts of interests which takes a very different approach.
Section 1090 of the Government Code Provides that,
"members of the . 
.district,. . . and city officers or
employees shall not be financially lnterested in any
contract made by them ln their official capacity, or by
any body or board of which they are rnembers. "
It then defines, "district" to include any
"agency of the state formed pursuant to general law or
special act, for the local performance of governmental or
proprietary functlons within limited boundaries."
In our opinion, this provision applies to members of the Board of
a Redevel.opment Agency. The only exception to this broad rule is
Iaid out in Section 1091, which says that an officer of the entity
is not considered to be interested in a contract entered into by
the District if the officer has only a remote interest in the
contract and, "if the fact of that interest is disclosed to the
body of the Board of which the officer is a member and noted in its
official records" and the Board then approves the contract. It
then goes on for thirteen separate sub-sections which define
"remote interest". 
None of these definitions seem to appty to
Board member A.
If Section 1090 applies to Board member A, then there is
no escape from its effect, Unlike provisions of the Fair political
Practices Act, in which di squal i f ication from participation in
decisions on that contract would meet the reguirements oi the code
provisions, Section 1090 does not have an easy method of complying
lrith the law. Under this statutory scheme, the contract woufa Ue


John Hendrickson, City Manager
January 24, l99L l
Yaqe/Ll
. * ,'tn*
,J"*vl'"1
voidable. {he only way of avoiding this effect wou}d be for either
(a) Board member A to resign from the Board or (b) sell his
interest in the partnership to a non-board member or (c) for the
partnership simply not to apply for the Ioan.
Case # 2
Two eye physicians have received their permit to buiId.
They then were told by Pac BeII that, in order to move a telephone
pole which was blocking placement of the driveway to the building,
Pac BelI would have to be paid in advance by the doctors. 
This
operation wiII cost over $8,000. The doctors have asked the Agency
to grant then the money to move the pole. 
The doctor's general
contractor on the project is Board member Neufeld. The moving of
the pole will actually be performed by someone other than Neufeld
or hls employees and the decision of the Agency in regard to the
grant wiII have no effect on Neufeld's contract.
1) 
Should Board member Neufeld remove himself from the meeting in
which this matter is discussed?
Short ansr"rer I It would probably be prudent but it may
not be req[irea. 
-
Discussion: Section 1090 of the Government Code apPlies
only to fi;E;;ial 
interest which Board members may have in
conlracts made by the Agency. This would not appear to apply in
this situatton. 
The Fair Political Practices Act applies only to
contracts in which the public official 
has a material financial
interest. 
As set forth in your statement of facts, Board member
Neufeld has no material financial interest in the contract between
the Agency and the doctors.
Hor.irever, the regulations of the Fair PoIiticaI Practices
Commission provide that the effect of a decision is material if,
"Any person (including a business entity) which has been
a source of income . . . to the official 
of $250 or more
in the preceding 12 months is directly involved in a
decision before the official's 
agency or there is a nexus
[as defined below] between the purpose for which the
official 
receives income and the governmental decision,
. . . There is a nexus between the purpose for which an
official 
receives income and a governmental decision if
the official receives income to achieve a goal or purpose
which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by
the decision. ,'
Arguably, in this case, Neufeld received income to achieve the goat
of bringing the construction of the building to a point where it
could be occupied. The decision of the Agency to grant the money


John Hendrickson, City I'lanager
January 24, 1991
Page 8
to the physicians would a.ide in bringing the construction of the
building to a polnt where it could be occupied. Admlttedly this is
a rather broad reading of the regulation. Holrever, I have seen the
rPPC read their reguLations even mole broadly than this'
Slnce, in this case, we are talking about only one member
of the Board being disqualified to act and, if he does not step
dolrn and the FPPC later determines that he should have, possible
criminal penaLties coutd be applied, then I would suggest erring on
the side of caution. Additionally, I assume that Mr. Neufeld has
been paid at least $250 by the doctors i,ithin the last 12 nonths.
Therefore, lf the first part of the above-quoted provlsion is read
separately, then the decision lrhether to grant the money is clearly
material. This analysis wouLd serve further to warant caution.
2l 
Again, can this natter even be acted upon given Dlrector
Neufeld's contract with the owners?
Short ansverr Yes.
Discussion: Because we are dealing only with the Palr
Polltica] FiEZuEEIect and noL with Sectlon 1090 of the Goverrunent
Code, drastic neasules are not required. Board member Neufeld can
step do\n'n and not paxti.cipate in making the decLslon. Thls would
leave more than enough neinbers of the Board wlthout a conflict of
interest who could then take action, If enough of the others also
had valid conflicts of interest so as to keep Lhe Agency fron being
abLe to have a quorum of Board nenbers n,ith no conflict of inuerest
Lhen. the rule of necessity says all of the members of the Board,
inctuding ur. Neufeld, cone back in and can act, so long as they
nake an oral disclosure of their confl-ict of interest before
partlclpating in the decision naking process.
3) 
Are there any other standards or rules the Board ought to be
aware of when confrontlng this type of situation?
Short answeri Yes.
Discussion: As noted above, the Board or the Clty should
have adoptEE-1E3-6wn conflict of interest code and should kaep that
code up to date. That nay have some relevant naterial in it.
Secondly, with this type of issue, different busLnesses or
professlons have lheir own sets of rules with regard to conflicLs
of interest which may or may not be applicable. For exanple,
building contractors are licensed by the State of California and
are heavily regulated, If they have a code ot professiona.l
conduct, tt may prohibit Director Neufeld fron acting both as
general contractor for the physicians and then acting as membet of
the Board of the RedeveLopment Agency in decidlng whether to fend
then enough noney to finish out the same project which he he.Lped
buiId.

John, I hope that the above information is ,tot too filled

Yes, the board members who do not,

sections.analysis:
The mere fact one or two of their members
has a con-iffEE--6f interest prohibits only that nember fron
participating in the decision. Members who do not have a conflicl
of interest themselves may partlclpate. No special procedures must
be follo\red. Ho$ever, if more than one Board menber has a conflict
then the i.ssue is whether there ale sufficient nernbers on the Board
who do not have a confllct of interest to constitute a quofum of
the Board. If a quolum remains. then that quorum can decide'. If
rln other words, lre do not count the conflicted nember for any
purpose, incfuding the establishment of a quorum. For example, -if
a five member board had two members declare a conflict of interest
and step down, and one of the other three nembers was no present
that night, the remaining two, non-conflicted menbers cannot act on
this agenda item because they lost their quorun when the two grho
had conflicts of j.nterest declared them, The rule of necessity
(continued.., )


John Hendrickson, City Manager
January 24, 1991
a quorum would not remain if -all of those with conflicts step down,
then the rule of necessity2 says alI of the members, in-luding
those with conflicts of interest, (whether direct or indirect), 
maY
participate in the decision after having made their oral disclosure
of the conflict of interest.
2l 
As described above, do the Board members who do not have a
direct interest in the project, have valid conflicts 
of
interest anyway? If not, what should they do in resolving
this issue?
Short answer: Board members A and D have a conflict of
interest but Board members B and C do not. If B and C continue to
maintain that they have a conflict of interest then no decision can
be made. The rule of necessity does not apply. If they agree that
they do not, then B, C and the other members of the Board, (other
than A and D), may act. 
However, please read the ans!'rer t.o (3)
below before taking any action.
Discussion: 
In our opinion, based on the following
discussionl--E6Eid--iember A, who is himself a partner, has a
conftict of interest in making a decision as to whether to lend the
partnership money to improve the real propert,y and, so long as he
1(...continued)
would not help under this set of facts, because there are
sufficient board members in office to make a decision, if they were
all present. Therefore, there is no choice but to continue the
matter .
2This rule is the product of FPPC decisions, Attorney General
opinions and some court decisions. It is based on a section of the
Fair PoIiticaI Practices Act which says that the Act,
"does not prevent any public officiaL from making or
participating in the making of a governmental decision to
the extent his participation is Iegally required for the
decision to be made. The fact that an official's 
vote is
needed to break a tie does not make his participation
IegaIIy required for purposes of this section. "
The regulations then interpret this to mean that the rule of
necess.i.ty applies if and only if, 
"there exists no alternative
source of decision consistent with the purposes and terms of the
statute authorizing the decision. " 
The regulation goes on to
specify that this rule is not to be construed to permit an official
vrho has a conflict of interest to vote merely to break a tj.e nor
permit him to vote,
"if a quorum can be convened of other members of the
agency who are not disqualified Iunder the Act] whether
oi not such other members are actually present at the
time of disqualification. "


John Hendrickson, City Manager
January 24, 19 9l
is either a partner in a business lrhich owns land in the Project
Area or owns land in the ProJect Area directly, wilf have conflicts
of interest with regard to many of the majol decision involving the
redevelopment proJect. Board member B, probably has no conf.Lict of
interest merely because of the partnership status of the Board
nember's nephew-in_law, once removed. If Board nember c has been
paid for the property in question and has no remaining legal
interest in the property, then Board member c probably has no
conflict of interest either, 
Board member D has a confLict of
interest only tf the loan to A's partnership !.rould have lriIl have
"no financial effect" ,hatsoever on D's property. Since the Loan
to A's partnership witl be used to develop the partnership's Iand,
it is clear that the action will have some financial effect on the
value of D's land, Therefore, D has a conflict of interest'
The term "conflict of lnterest" is nost easily defined by
the Fair Polittcal Practices Act. Section 87100 of the Governnent
Code provides:
"No public officiat at any 1eveI of state or loca]
government shall nake, participate in makinq or ln any
way attenpt to use his officiaL position to lnfluence a
governnental deci.sion in vrhich he kno\rs or has reason to
know he has a financial lnterest."
The tern "pubtic officlal at any level of state or 1oca1
government'i, is defined 1n the legulatlons t{hich are found at TitIe
2, california Administratlve code, Seqtion 18?00, et' seq. The
tern is defined to mean, "every natural person i,ho is a menber,
offlcer, enployee or consultant of a state or local goverrulent
agency.'r Clearty a clty council mernber or a member of the Board of
Directors of a Redevelopnent Agency is a "publlc official" within
this de flnition.
The tern, "nakes a govelnmental decision" is defined to
include voting, appointing a person, enteling into a contract on
behalf of t.he agency or "obllgates or coNnits his or her agency to
any course of aation." It can aLso include a declslon !g! to take
any of these actions, unless the dec-ision noL to act is made
because of the financial interest, "l'lhen the deternination not to
act occurs because of his or her financial j.nterest. the official's
determination must be accompanied by discLosure of the financial
interest, made part of the agency's official record or made in
writing to the, 
." to the appropriate pefson. Again, any formal
action by the Board of the RedeveLopment Agency which affected this
property would probably be considered the making of a governrnental
decls 1on.
The term "attemptinq to use his official position to
influence", is defined in the regulations to include, "for Lhe
purpose of influencing the decision, the offlclal contacts, or


John Hendrickson, City Manager
January 24, 1991
appears before, or other wise attempts to influence, any member,
officer, 
employee or consultant of the agency," in which he is an
official 
or over which the agency in which he is an official 
has
budgetary control.
"Attempting to influence", also includes "aPpearance or
contact by the official 
on behalf of a business entity, client, or
customer. " So, in other words, if Board member A, who is a nember
of the partnership which own the property were to even contact
empLoyees or other Board members on behalf of the partnership, he
would be "attempting to use his official position Iwith the Agency]
to influence" a decision which the Agency was making in regard to
the property. 
The language of this regulation is so broad that
many attorneys advise the affected public official to at least step
down from the dias where the other members are discussing the
matter and some even advise the person to leave the room entirely
in order to avoid the appearance of "attempting to influence" the
decis ion.
The term "material financial effect" is defined in the
regulations based on the nature of the interest'
Boardmember A: Under the regulations adopted Pursuant to
the Fair P6ITEGI Practices Act, the decision is said to have a
material financial effect if "any business entity in which the
officlal 
is. 
.Ia] partner. 
.is directly involved in a decision
before the officiat's 
agency." 
If the official 
owned land
directly, then the decision is said to have a material financial
effect if ,
"the decision is to 
adopt the redevelopnent plan,
to add terrltory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind
or amend any of the above decisions; and real property in
which the official 
has an interest, or any part of it is
located within 
the boundaries (or 
the 
proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment area."
Similarly, if the official 
owned the land directly involved in the
decision, then if 
the decision involved the imposition or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed on the property or if
the decision involved the revocation or denial of a license or
permit or land use entitlement authorizing a specific use of the
property then the decision clearly has a material financial effect
on the property.
Boardmember B:
The general rule contained in the
regulations in regard to whether a decision has a "material
financial effect", is that the decision does have such an effect,
"if the decision will have a significant effect on
the official 
or a member of the official's 
immediate


John Hendrickson, City Manager
January 24, I99 L
family, or on the source of income, the source of gifts,
the business entity, or the real property, which is an
economic interest of the official."
Additionat provisions state that the effect of a decision is
material if that decisi.on wiII result in the
"personal expenses, income, assets (other than interest
in real property), or liabilities 
of the official 
or his
or her immediate family increasing or decreasing by at
least $250. "
Repeatedly, the regulations, when they refer to anyone other than
the official 
himself or herself, the reference is to "his or her
immediate family. " Unfortunately, the regulations never define vrho
is within an "immediate famiIy". 
The fact that the adJective
"immediate" was used tends to mean to me that someone as remote as
a nephe!,r- in- Iar,r, once renoved, was not intended to be included.
Certainly if the people in question are adults who do not reside in
the same household, this remote a relationship would not come
within the scope of the term "immediate family", in my opinion.
Boardmember D:
Board nember D owns. If it
appIy. If it is not his
You do not say what kind of ProPerty
is his residence then one set of rules
residence then another set applies.
First, I will assume that it $ras not his residence:
A decision is said to have a "material financiaf effect"
on property indirectty involved in the decision lf the effect of
the decision is material as to real property which the official has
a direct interest in and, one of the following applies: (a) the
property is withln a 300 foot radius of the boundaries of the
property whlch is the subject of the decislon, "un.Less the decision
will have no fLnancial effect upon the official's real property
interest". or (b) the real property is outside of the 300 foot
radius but within a 2,500 foot radius and the decision wiII have a
reasonably foreseeable financial effect of $10,000 more on the
market value of the official's land, or of $1,000 or more in annual
rental value of the official's land. If the land is more than
2,500 feet from the subject property then the decision is presumed
by Iaw to have no material financial effect on the official's
property.
If the property is D's residence then a special rule
applies. Because the City has a population of less than 25,000
people and covers a geographic area of ten square miles, or Less,
and the Council members are required to reside in the jurisdiction,
gnq, are elected at large and we are talking about an indirect
benefit to D's property, theh we can ignore the 2,500 foot radius
issue. Hor"/ever, the 300 foot radius would stil1 app1y. Since you


John Hendrickson, City Manager
January 24, 799 7
say thaL D's property is "next door", I assume that it is .Less than
300 feet away.
3) 
Are there any other standards or rules the Board ought to be
aware of when confronting this type of situation?
Short answer: Yes.
Discussion: All of the above analysis is based on the
fair politIEEI practices Act and the regulations adopted pursuant
to it. 
There are, however, two other sources of binding 1aw and
regul-ations on the subject of conflicts of interest. First of aI1,
other provisions of the Fair Political Practices Act require the
City to have adopted its ovrn conflict of j.nterest code. Some codes
adopted by cities merely recite provisions of the Act, other have
their own substantive rules. I do not know if your City or the
Agency by itself have adopted any such code.
Secondly, the Government Code contains another chapter on
conflicts of interests which takes a very different approach.
Section 1090 of the Government Code Provides that,
"members of the . 
.district,. . . and city officers or
employees shall not be financially lnterested in any
contract made by them ln their official capacity, or by
any body or board of which they are rnembers. "
It then defines, "district" to include any
"agency of the state formed pursuant to general law or
special act, for the local performance of governmental or
proprietary functlons within limited boundaries."
In our opinion, this provision applies to members of the Board of
a Redevel.opment Agency. The only exception to this broad rule is
Iaid out in Section 1091, which says that an officer of the entity
is not considered to be interested in a contract entered into by
the District if the officer has only a remote interest in the
contract and, "if the fact of that interest is disclosed to the
body of the Board of which the officer is a member and noted in its
official records" and the Board then approves the contract. It
then goes on for thirteen separate sub-sections which define
"remote interest". 
None of these definitions seem to appty to
Board member A.
If Section 1090 applies to Board member A, then there is
no escape from its effect, Unlike provisions of the Fair political
Practices Act, in which di squal i f ication from participation in
decisions on that contract would meet the reguirements oi the code
provisions, Section 1090 does not have an easy method of complying
lrith the law. Under this statutory scheme, the contract woufa Ue


John Hendrickson, City Manager
January 24, l99L l
Yaqe/Ll
. * ,'tn*
,J"*vl'"1
voidable. {he only way of avoiding this effect wou}d be for either
(a) Board member A to resign from the Board or (b) sell his
interest in the partnership to a non-board member or (c) for the
partnership simply not to apply for the Ioan.
Case # 2
Two eye physicians have received their permit to buiId.
They then were told by Pac BeII that, in order to move a telephone
pole which was blocking placement of the driveway to the building,
Pac BelI would have to be paid in advance by the doctors. 
This
operation wiII cost over $8,000. The doctors have asked the Agency
to grant then the money to move the pole. 
The doctor's general
contractor on the project is Board member Neufeld. The moving of
the pole will actually be performed by someone other than Neufeld
or hls employees and the decision of the Agency in regard to the
grant wiII have no effect on Neufeld's contract.
1) 
Should Board member Neufeld remove himself from the meeting in
which this matter is discussed?
Short ansr"rer I It would probably be prudent but it may
not be req[irea. 
-
Discussion: Section 1090 of the Government Code apPlies
only to fi;E;;ial 
interest which Board members may have in
conlracts made by the Agency. This would not appear to apply in
this situatton. 
The Fair Political Practices Act applies only to
contracts in which the public official 
has a material financial
interest. 
As set forth in your statement of facts, Board member
Neufeld has no material financial interest in the contract between
the Agency and the doctors.
Hor.irever, the regulations of the Fair PoIiticaI Practices
Commission provide that the effect of a decision is material if,
"Any person (including a business entity) which has been
a source of income . . . to the official 
of $250 or more
in the preceding 12 months is directly involved in a
decision before the official's 
agency or there is a nexus
[as defined below] between the purpose for which the
official 
receives income and the governmental decision,
. . . There is a nexus between the purpose for which an
official 
receives income and a governmental decision if
the official receives income to achieve a goal or purpose
which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by
the decision. ,'
Arguably, in this case, Neufeld received income to achieve the goat
of bringing the construction of the building to a point where it
could be occupied. The decision of the Agency to grant the money


John Hendrickson, City I'lanager
January 24, 1991
to the physicians would a.ide in bringing the construction of the
building to a polnt where it could be occupied. Admlttedly this is
a rather broad reading of the regulation. Holrever, I have seen the
rPPC read their reguLations even mole broadly than this'
Slnce, in this case, we are talking about only one member
of the Board being disqualified to act and, if he does not step
dolrn and the FPPC later determines that he should have, possible
criminal penaLties coutd be applied, then I would suggest erring on
the side of caution. Additionally, I assume that Mr. Neufeld has
been paid at least $250 by the doctors i,ithin the last 12 nonths.
Therefore, lf the first part of the above-quoted provlsion is read
separately, then the decision lrhether to grant the money is clearly
material. This analysis wouLd serve further to warant caution.
2l 
Again, can this natter even be acted upon given Dlrector
Neufeld's contract with the owners?
Short ansverr Yes.
Discussion: Because we are dealing only with the Palr
Polltica] FiEZuEEIect and noL with Sectlon 1090 of the Goverrunent
Code, drastic neasules are not required. Board member Neufeld can
step do\n'n and not paxti.cipate in making the decLslon. Thls would
leave more than enough neinbers of the Board wlthout a conflict of
interest who could then take action, If enough of the others also

abLe to have a quorum of Board nenbers n,ith no conflict of inuerest
Lhen. the rule of necessity says all of the members of the Board,
inctuding ur. Neufeld, cone back in and can act, so long as they
nake an oral disclosure of their confl-ict of interest before
partlclpating in the decision naking process.
3) 
Are there any other standards or rules the Board ought to be
aware of when confrontlng this type of situation?
Short answeri Yes.
Discussion: As noted above, the Board or the Clty should
have adoptEE-1E3-6wn conflict of interest code and should kaep that
code up to date. That nay have some relevant naterial in it.
Secondly, with this type of issue, different busLnesses or
professlons have lheir own sets of rules with regard to conflicLs
of interest which may or may not be applicable. For exanple,
building contractors are licensed by the State of California and
are heavily regulated, If they have a code ot professiona.l
conduct, tt may prohibit Director Neufeld fron acting both as
general contractor for the physicians and then acting as membet of
the Board of the RedeveLopment Agency in decidlng whether to fend
then enough noney to finish out the same project which he he.Lped
buiId.


John Hendrickson, City l{anager
January 24, 1991
